coredata.dc:title: Novel thermal quenching characteristics of luminescent carbon nanodots via tailoring the surface chemical groups
coredata.dc:creator: Maiti, R.
Mukherjee, S.
Haldar, S.
Bhowmick, D.
Ray, S.K.
coredata.dc:description: We report the novel thermal quenching of carbon nanodots (CNDs), synthesized by a simple solvothermal method. The emission energy and intensity of the CND samples can be tuned by thermal annealing at varying temperatures in an ambient atmosphere, attributed to the varying fractions of the core sp2 moiety and the surrounding surface chemical groups. While the as-synthesized samples show a positive thermal quenching of luminescence, the samples annealed at 200 °C show novel negative thermal quenching of emission with increasing measurement temperature. To the best of our knowledge, we report for the first time the observation of positive to negative tuning of thermal quenching behavior, in the temperature range 10 K–300 K, for annealed CND samples. A schematic energy band diagram model has been presented to explain this novel phenomenon. Time resolved emission characteristics of CND samples studied using time correlated single photon counting technique indicate a significant change of lifetime of annealed CND samples.

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Council of Scientific and Industrial Research, India

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is supported by the funding from the GBH project, sponsored by CSIR, Government of India ​03(1275)/13/EMR-II dt. 12.04 13 . The use of the XPS facility from the DST-FIST program of the Department of Physics is acknowledged.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Typical TEM micrograph of as-synthesized CNDs in solution. Histogram of lateral size distribution of 100 randomly selected CNDs from the TEM image showing an average size of ∼1.8 ± 0.2 nm (inset), (b) HRTEM image of corresponding CNDs and. SAED pattern showing the amorphous nature (inset) (c) Typical AFM image of CNDs coated on Si substrate. (d) The corresponding height distribution of CNDs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) The absorption spectrum of as-synthesized CNDs in aqueous solution exhibiting single peak around 270 nm corresponding to π–π* transition. (Inset shows a photograph of CNDs in aqueous dispersions) and (b) X-ray photoelectron spectra showing the evolution of oxygenated functional groups of CNDs as a function of anneal temperature. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Room temperature photoluminescence spectra of the different CND samples on Si substrates under illumination with 325 nm. Inset shows the photographs of emission color from four samples. (b) Typical PL spectrum of as-synthesized (sample 1) is deconvoluted into two Gaussian-like peaks centered at ∼445 nm (peak-1) and at ∼510 nm (peak-2). (c) The shift of the PL energy of peak-1 and -2 with anneal temperature. (d) The shift of the full width half maxima (FWHM) of peak-1 and -2 with anneal temperature. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Temperature dependent PL spectrum of sample 1 on Si measured in a range 10 K–300 K (b) Temperature dependent PL spectra of sample 5 on Si annealed at 200 °C (c) Integrated PL intensity of CND samples on as a function of temperature for different annealing conditions. (d) PL spectra normalized integrated intensity area of CND samples annealed at different temperatures compared and fitted by Equation (1). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Schematic energy band diagrams for (a) sample 1 and (b) sample 5. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Time resolved PL decay characteristic of (a) samples 1, 3, 5 acquired at 445 nm emission wavelength and (b) samples 1, 3, 5 acquired at 510, 520, 540 nm, respectively. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Evolution of sp2 hybridized C and different oxygenated functional groups.
Fitting parameters of PL intensity for samples annealed at different temperatures.
Fitting Parameters for Fig. 6, showing average lifetime for core and surface state emission.

head.ce:title: Novel thermal quenching characteristics of luminescent carbon nanodots via tailoring the surface chemical groups

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Indian Institute of Technology Kharagpur, Kharagpur 721302, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Advanced Technology Development Centre, Indian Institute of Technology, Kharagpur 721302, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We report the novel thermal quenching of carbon nanodots (CNDs), synthesized by a simple solvothermal method. The emission energy and intensity of the CND samples can be tuned by thermal annealing at varying temperatures in an ambient atmosphere, attributed to the varying fractions of the core sp2 moiety and the surrounding surface chemical groups. While the as-synthesized samples show a positive thermal quenching of luminescence, the samples annealed at 200 °C show novel negative thermal quenching of emission with increasing measurement temperature. To the best of our knowledge, we report for the first time the observation of positive to negative tuning of thermal quenching behavior, in the temperature range 10 K–300 K, for annealed CND samples. A schematic energy band diagram model has been presented to explain this novel phenomenon. Time resolved emission characteristics of CND samples studied using time correlated single photon counting technique indicate a significant change of lifetime of annealed CND samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Several novel properties of different forms of carbon such as fullerenes, carbon nanotubes, nano diamonds, and graphene sheets have been actively explored [1]. Carbon nanodots (CNDs), the youngest member of the nano-carbon family, have attracted attention since the first report in 2004 [2] due to their excellent photostability, small size, biocompatibility, and highly tunable emission with high quantum yield [3–5]. Because of their intriguing properties, CNDs are promising in a broad range of applications like sensors [6,7], photovoltaics, light-emitting diodes (LEDs) [8–10], biomedical imaging, catalysis, energy storage etc. [11–13], One of the unique aspects of CNDs is the excitation dependent photoluminescence emission characteristics [14]. However, their photoluminescence (PL) spectrum generally exhibits a large full width at half-maximum (FWHM) as compared to inorganic semiconductor quantum dots. In general, the enhancement of CND luminescence can be gained through surface passivation processes, doping by organic polymers, coupling to metal-based nanostructures, etc. [15,16], Kang et al. have shown the tunability of PL emission across the UV-VIS-NIR wavelength range by varying the size of CNDs [17]. Mao and co-workers have obtained tunable PL emission in the visible region, which was attributed to the surface oxidation of CNDs [18]. In general, the origin of PL emission from CNDs can be attributed mainly to the sp2 hybridized core states and the surrounding surface defect states. Much of the recent work has focused on different synthesis procedure to obtain higher quantum yield, but the exact physical origin of such excellent emission properties is still unclear [19,20].

Here, we report for the first time the novel and tunable thermal quenching characteristics of highly luminescent CNDs. The CNDs synthesized via solvo-thermal treatment of orange juice in ethanol show a broad emission in the visible range from 400 to 700 nm, which can be tuned by varying the surface chemical groups. As-synthesized CNDs show typical positive thermal quenching (PTQ) effect, whereas annealing at higher temperatures under ambient conditions gives rise to novel negative thermal quenching (NTQ) phenomena. Time resolved PL decay measurements have been carried out to explain the mechanism for thermal quenching.

Solvothermal carbonization process followed by extraction using various organic precursors is a popular method to synthesize carbon nanodots due to the low cost, ease of fabrication, environmental friendliness and nontoxicity of the method [21,22]. Typically, an organic reagent is reacted with a carbon containing precursor in a hydrothermal reactor at high temperatures to synthesize CNDs. Here, we report a very simple one step synthesis process of highly luminescent CNDs via solvothermal treatment of orange juice in ethanol, followed by washing and gradient centrifugation. We first poured pure ethanol solvent in absolutely pulp free orange juice solution in a ratio of 1:1 (v/v) and mixed them well using a magnetic stirrer at a speed of 500 rpm. The solution was then kept in a teflon autoclave and heated at ∼120 °C for 2.5 h. At higher temperature and pressure, the dehydration and decomposition of fructose/glucose take place, resulting in different soluble products, e.g., furfural compounds, organic acids (such as acetic, propionic, levulinic acids), aldehydes, phenols, etc. The catalytic activity of hydronium ions present in some weak acids like citric acid and ascorbic acid, in our natural precursor, controlled the dehydration and decomposition reaction. Subsequent aldol condensation, cycloaddition, hydroxymethyl mediated furan resin condensation result in aromatization and formation of aromatic clusters [21,23]. After the completion of reaction, the autoclave was left to cool down naturally to room temperature. The resultant dark yellowish solution was washed using excess dichloromethane for 3–4 times to remove any residual unreacted organic molecules. The solution was mixed with acetone and then centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 15 min to separate out comparatively larger sized CNDs. The supernatant was decanted and again centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 15 min to obtain smaller sized CNDs. The precipitate, in general, can be re-dispersed into other solvents like, ethanol, water, toluene etc., for further processing. The supernatant liquid was collected separately, which consisted of very small sized CNDs (yield ∼7%) dispersed in acetone.

The surface morphology of synthesized CNDs was studied using a high resolution transmission electron microscope (JEOL JEM-2100F) and scanning probe microscope (Veeco Nanoscope III). The evolution of the chemical functional groups of the CNDs film was investigated using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (PHI 5000 Versa Probe II, ULVAC-PHI, INC., Japan) with an incident Al Kα X-ray of energy 1486.6 eV. The UV–VIS absorption spectrum of CNDs was recorded using a fiber probe based UV–VIS–NIR CCD spectrometer.

For photoluminescence study, we drop casted the synthesized CNDs dispersion on top of a Si substrate. Five sets of samples including as synthesized (sample 1) and samples annealed at 75 °C (sample 2), 100 °C (sample 3), 150 °C (sample 4) and 200 °C (sample 5) were prepared for PL investigation. After drop-casting, four samples (samples 2 to 5) were annealed in air at different temperatures varying from 75 to 200 °C for 1 h. We acquired the temperature dependent steady state PL spectra using a closed cycle cryostat (JANIS, model CCS-150) Spectra were acquired by using a HeCd laser of wavelength 325 nm as the excitation source and a TRIAX-320 monochromator fitted with Hamamatsu R928 photomultiplier detector. Time resolved PL characteristics of CND dispersions were recorded at room temperature using a LifeSpec II fluorescence lifetime spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments) fitted with a photomultiplier tube (PMT) detector and a diode laser with 100 μW average power at 375 nm (EPL-375).

A typical transmission electron microscope (TEM) image of CNDs, prepared by solvo-thermal process, representing the formation of spherical nano-dots is shown in Fig. 1 a. The average size of the CNDs is found to be ∼1.8 nm from the size distribution histogram shown in the inset of Fig. 1a. Corresponding high resolution TEM (HRTEM) micrograph shown in Fig. 1b reveals that the synthesized CNDs are almost spherical in shape having an average lateral diameter of ∼2 nm. The selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern (inset of Fig. 1b) indicates the poor crystalline nature of CNDs, which can be attributed as the high amorphous fraction in small sized dots due to the presence of different oxygenated functional groups on their surfaces. Fig. 1c displays the typical atomic force microscopy (AFM) image of CNDs and the corresponding height distribution with an average height of ∼3 nm is shown in Fig. 1d. The observation is in close agreement with the TEM results.

Fig. 2 a shows the absorption spectrum of as-synthesized CNDs (AS) recorded in an aqueous solution. The spectrum exhibits a single peak at 270 nm. The spectrum extends up to 800 nm without any other significant features. The peak at 270 nm is due to π–π* transition of aromatic >CC< bonds. X-ray photo electron spectra (XPS) showing the binding energy of C1s electrons of CNDs annealed at two different temperatures (100 and 200 °C) are shown in Fig. 2b. The spectrum of the as-synthesized control sample is also shown for comparison. A gradual modification of the surface chemical states of CNDs with increasing annealing temperature is clearly observed on deconvolution of XPS spectra. XPS peaks from C1s spectra of as-synthesized CNDs are ascribed to CC or CC bonds at 284.6 eV, epoxy (CO) or hydroxyl (COH) bonds at 286.4 eV and carboxyl (COOH) bonds at 288.8 eV, in agreement with previous reports [24,25]. The spectra exhibit an increase of oxygenated chemical groups at the CND surface with increasing anneal temperature, which play a crucial role to tune the emission across the visible wavelength spectrum (to be discussed later). For the as-synthesized sample, the fraction of surface defect states is evidently much lower than that of sp2 hybridized (CC) states. With increase in anneal temperature, the surface oxidation states of CNDs are modified gradually, which is depicted clearly in Table 1 . For higher temperature (200 °C) annealed sample, the spectrum reveals a considerable increase in oxygenated functional groups.

Synthesized carbon nanodots coated on Si substrates exhibit tunable photoluminescence emission across the visible wavelength spectrum (360–750 nm). The emission wavelength could be tuned by varying only the surface chemical states of CNDs. The evolution of PL emission spectra of CNDs as a function of anneal temperature is shown in Fig. 3 a. Photographs in the inset of Fig. 3a reveal a gradual transformation of emission color from blue to red with the increase of anneal temperature in the ambient condition. It is interesting to note that the intensity of PL emission for the annealed samples is lower than the as-synthesized CNDs. The PL. is completely quenched for the sample annealed at 300 °C (not shown here). This is attributed to the significant increase in surface trap states (non-radiative recombination centers) with the gradual evolution of hydroxyl, epoxy and carboxyl functional groups as the annealing temperature is increased. This result thus exhibits a strong correlation of the PL intensity with the increased surface oxygenated chemical states and decreased sp2 fractions as a function of temperature, in corroboration with the XPS measurements.

Fig. 3b shows a typical PL spectrum of as-synthesized CNDs drop-casted on top of the Si substrate. The PL spectrum is very broad ranging from 350 nm to 800 nm. The spectrum is mainly composed of two Gaussian peaks, the first one (peak-1) is at ∼445 nm and the second one (peak- 2) is at ∼510 nm. The shift of the energy and full-width-half-maxima (FWHM) of PL peaks with the variation of annealing temperature is shown in Fig. 3c and d, respectively. The error bars in Fig. 3c have been calculated using the standard deviation of the data of five experimental replicas for 3 sets of samples. With the increase in annealing temperature, the energy for peak-1 exhibits a negligible change, while that for peak-2 is gradually red-shifted from 515 nm to 560 nm. The FWHM of peak-1 gradually decreases but that for peak-2 shows an opposite behavior as anneal temperature in increased (Fig. 3d). The emission from the CND samples mainly constitute of two peaks. One is due to the core state emission (∼445 nm) and other one due to the surface state emission (∼500–560 nm). For as-synthesized sample, the peak-1 emission dominates over the surface state emission. The change in dimension of the core molecule with increasing temperature is very low due to the very small (∼10−6 K−1) coefficient of thermal expansion of CC, CC bonds [26]. Thus, the red shift of peak-1 due to the increase in core size is insignificant. However, the fraction (84%–52%) of sp2 hybridized carbon (CC) is reduced in core moiety leading to the gradual decrease of FWHM of the peak-1 (from 80 nm to 18 nm) with increasing anneal temperature. On the other hand, for higher anneal temperature, the density of functional groups increases (Table 1), resulting in the red shifted emission of peak-2 as shown in Fig. 3c. XPS measurements also indicated the increase of various functional groups with anneal temperature. This leads to a significant decrease of the fraction of the smaller core moieties causing reduced intensity of peak-1. An increase in the fraction of oxidized shell of CNDs leads to a huge increase in FWHM from 15 nm to 168 nm. The broader peak consists of emission from several functional groups including >CO, COOH, COH, COC, and CONH2. The contribution of this peak nevertheless gains with respect to the sp2 peak at a higher annealing temperature. The dominating PL peak for higher temperature annealed sample is thus related to surface state emission. The functional groups have various energy levels, giving rise to a series of emissive traps [27,28]. A possible origin for the tunable emission is that the initial excitation is followed by energy transfer from the higher energy absorbing states to a lower energy emissive site in annealed CND samples.

For further probing of the PL emission mechanism of CNDs, we have carried out temperature-dependent measurements from 10 to 300 K for samples annealed at different temperatures. The temperature-dependent intensity is useful to understand the nature of radiative transitions [29]. Temperature dependent PL spectra for as-synthesized CNDs sample are shown in Fig. 4 a. As the measurement temperature increases, the PL intensity decreases gradually, which is attributed to thermally activated non-radiative trapping of excited carriers. At higher temperatures, the overwhelming majority of excited electrons relax through non-radiative pathways, viz., surface trap states, defect states or ionized impurity states. This can be expressed by τnr = C exp (Ea/KBT), where τnr is the non-radiative lifetime, C is a constant and Ea is the activation energy. The quantum efficiency is generally expressed as η = (1 + τr/τnr)−1, where τr is radiative lifetime of carriers. The non-radiative lifetime decreases with increasing temperature. This leads to a decrease in the quantum efficiency and PL intensity at a higher temperature. In contrast to the as-synthesized sample, CNDs annealed at higher temperatures show an anomalous temperature dependent PL behavior. Fig. 4b shows an increase of PL intensity with the increase of measurement temperature for the sample 5, annealed at 200 °C. This behavior is opposite to that observed in semiconductor quantum dots and is known as negative thermal quenching (NTQ) effect. The unique NTQ characteristics are being reported by us, for the first time, in luminescent CNDs. Samples 2 & 3, annealed at 75 and 100 °C, respectively display a transition from PTQ to NTQ characteristics in between 10 and 300 K. The intensity initially decreases with the increase of temperature but above a certain temperature it increases again as shown in Fig. 4c. It is quite clear from the results, that the samples annealed at higher temperatures show negative thermal quenching phenomena, whereas, as-synthesized CNDs exhibit PTQ behavior like other semiconductor nanocrystals. But, samples annealed at intermediate temperatures (75 and 100 °C) exhibit a gradual transition from PTQ to NTQ phenomena.

To explain the results quantitatively, we have used the following multi-level model reported by Shibata [30], which is expressed as
(1)
I
( T )
= I
( 0 )
1 +
∑
q = 1
w
D q
exp
(
−
E q ′
k B
T
)
1 +
∑
p = 1
m
C p
exp
(
−
E p
k B
T
)

Here, I(T) is the PL intensity at an arbitrary temperature T, I(0) is intensity at 0 K, k
B is the Boltzmann constant, E
p
(E
q
′ ) are the activation energy of the processes that leads to a decrease (increase) of the PL intensity with temperature, and C
p
(D
q
) is the corresponding weight factor. The exponential terms in the denominator refer to the normal temperature quenching process, while those in the numerator describe the negative thermal quenching effect. All fitting results of Equation (1) to the experimental are listed in Table 2 and the fitted plots are shown in Fig. 4d for three different samples.

To have more insight about the PL behavior, the schematic energy band diagram model is presented in Fig. 5 . Fig. 5a and b represent the schematic energy band diagram for as-synthesized and annealed CND samples (200 °C), respectively, to explain the tunable PL emissions and thermal quenching behavior. The PTQ and NTQ characteristics are attributed to (and hence proportional to) the fraction of the core states and the surface states, respectively. For as-synthesized CNDs, the emission is dominated by the core states as shown in Fig. 5a. Hence, at lower temperatures, the defect frozen effect and the hopping mechanism among sp2 clusters result in a typical PTQ behavior [31]. The activation energies are found to be 14.14 and 1.74 meV for increased occurrence of non-radiative processes (Table 2). But, for the surface group emission, at higher anneal temperature with relatively higher fraction of oxygenated functional.

groups, the exciton migration from sp2 clusters to sp3 defect sites or disorder-induced localized states [32] may be the dominant mechanism. Incident photons excite a huge number of electrons into LUMO. There is also some thermal contribution of carriers in LUMO at a higher measurement temperature. For the annealed samples, the excited electrons relax back primarily through non-radiative pathways and are trapped in states just below (∼meV) the LUMO band (Fig. 5b). Thus the PL intensity is low at lower temperatures. However, with increasing measurement temperature, the trapped electrons residing in energy levels within the band gap are thermally excited into radiative channels after overcoming the activation energy of 12.13 and 220.50 meV (Table 2). Hence PL intensity increases with increase in measurement temperature. This results in the observed NTQ behavior for annealed samples. It is to be noted that sample 2 exhibits both PTQ and NTQ effects with the corresponding activation energy of 0.53 meV and 78.67 meV, respectively. The last column in Table 2 shows the decreasing transition point from PTQ to NTQ with increasing annealing temperature. This is due to the dominance of NTQ over PTQ with increased fraction of surface functional groups.

We have used the time correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) technique for the PL lifetime measurements to further understand the emission mechanism. The time resolved PL emission spectra, shown in Fig. 6 a and b, have been obtained by using an excitation wavelength of 375 nm and a 415 nm long pass filter. The time decay profile for core state and surface state related emissions shown in Fig. 6 have been fitted using tri-exponential function, given by
(2)
I
( t )
=
B 1
e
− t
/
τ 1
+
B 2
e
− t
/
τ 2
+
B 3
e
− t
/
τ 3

Where τ
1
, τ
2
, τ
3 are respectively the decay lifetimes of luminescence and B
1
, B
2
, B
3 are the corresponding weight factors. Time decay profiles for core state transition for all the CND samples are recorded at 445 nm (Fig. 6a) and surface state related transitions (Fig. 6b) are recorded at 510 nm, 520 nm and 540 nm for the samples 1, 3 and 5, respectively. The average lifetime of the decay process has been calculated using,
(3)
τ
a v
=
B 1
τ 1 2
+
B 2
τ 2 2
+
B 3
τ 3 2
B 1
τ 1
+
B 2
τ 2
+
B 3
τ 3

It is quite evident from Table 3 is that the average PL decay lifetime is found to be decreased for the samples annealed at higher temperatures. The average lifetime (τav) is found to be 4.58, 2.06 and 1.73 for core state emission of samples 1, 3 and 5, respectively. The surface state emission, probed at three different emission wavelengths for three different samples, shows the average lifetime to be 4.22, 2.62 and 0.99 for samples 1, 3 and 5, respectively. This could be attributed to higher probability of non-radiative transitions, which become possible due to the formation of non-radiative scattering centers at a higher annealing temperature. Hence, with increasing anneal temperature, the probability of non-radiative transition will be higher causing a faster decay of carriers. The multiple decay times in the expression indicates the complexity of the decay process, whereas semiconductor quantum dots generally depict a single exponential decay [33]. The fitted parameters from Fig. 6a and b are presented in Table 3, for the core and surface state emissions, respectively.

We have demonstrated that the PL emission from highly luminescent CNDs can be tuned over the visible range by modulating the surface chemical groups via thermal annealing. While the core emission energy of CNDs is weakly dependent on anneal temperature, the emission energy and intensity of oxygenated functional groups are found to be strongly temperature dependent. Temperature dependent PL measurements exhibit the previously unobserved tunable thermal quenching behavior for synthesized CND samples. As-synthesized samples show positive thermal quenching of PL, while the samples annealed at higher temperatures show a novel negative thermal quenching behavior. A mixed behavior of PTQ and NTQ is observed for the samples annealed at intermediate temperatures. The mechanism behind this unique phenomenon is explained by a schematic energy band diagram. Time resolved PL decay measurements have been carried out to find out the life time of excited carriers for different CND samples. Further research into this novel phenomenon may lead to the use of tunable and low-cost carbon nanodots as the fluorescent sources for future optoelectronic systems.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the funding from the GBH project, sponsored by CSIR, Government of India ​03(1275)/13/EMR-II dt. 12.04 13. The use of the XPS facility from the DST-FIST program of the Department of Physics is acknowledged.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Broad family of carbon nanoallotropes: classification, chemistry, and applications of fullerenes, carbon dots, nanotubes, graphene, nanodiamonds, and combined superstructures
Electrophoretic analysis and purification of fluorescent single-walled carbon nanotube fragments
Analytical and bioanalytical applications of carbon dots
Graphene quantum dots: emergent nanolights for bioimaging, sensors, catalysis and photovoltaic devices
Sensing metal ions with ion selectivity of crown ether and fluorescence resonance energy transfer between carbon dots and grapheme
Carbon nanodots as fluorescence probes for rapid, sensitive, and label-free detection of Hg2+and biothiols in complex matrices
Synthesis of direct white-light emitting carbogenic quantum dots
White light-emitting devices based on carbon dots' electroluminescence
Carbon dots as nontoxic and high-performance fluorescence imaging agents
Surface chemistry routes to modulate the photoluminescence of graphene quantum dots: from fluorescence mechanism to up-conversion bioimaging applications
Carbon quantum dot coated Mn3O4 with enhanced performances for lithium-ion batteries
An aqueous route to multicolor photoluminescent carbon dots using silica spheres as carriers
Deep ultraviolet photoluminescence of water-soluble self-passivated graphene quantum dots
Water-soluble fluorescent carbon quantum dots and photocatalyst design
Fluorescent carbon nanoparticles derived from candle soot
Quantum-sized carbon dots for bright and colorful photoluminescence
Simple one-step synthesis of highly luminescent carbon dots from orange juice application as excellent bio-imaging agents
One-step ultrasonic synthesis of water-soluble carbon nanoparticles with excellent photoluminescent properties
A green and facile approach for the synthesis of water soluble fluorescent carbon dots from banana juice
Synthesis of graphene-based nanosheets via chemical reduction of exfoliated graphite oxide
Tunable optical properties of graphene oxide by tailoring the oxygen functionalities using infrared irradiation
Structural and optical properties of N-doped graphene quantum dots
Polyethyleneimine-functionalized fluorescent carbon dots: water stability, pH sensing, and cellular imaging
Enhanced order and electronic delocalization in conjugated polymers oriented by gel processing in polyethylene
Negative thermal quenching curves in photoluminescence of solids
Mn2+-mediated energy transfer process as a versatile origin of photoluminescence in graphene oxide
Temperature dependence of the photoluminescence properties of colloidal CdSe/ZnS core/shell quantum dots embedded in a polystyrene matrix
